{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|12=draft}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=draft}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|12=draft}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都|12=draft}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|12=draft}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=draft}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|12=draft}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|12=draft}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川|12=draft}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=draft}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|12=draft}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=draft}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=draft}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=draft}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=draft}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|12=draft}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|12=draft}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|12=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|12=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|12=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.

The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|12=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=draft}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|12=draft}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=draft}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|12=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|12=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== References ==
<references />
== External Links == * [https://dailyportalz.jp/b/2007/05/09/c/ Myōkenjima on Daily Portal Z] * [https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/24985/ Urban Life Metro article on Myōkenjima]

revision.text:
{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|12=draft|12=draft}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=draft|12=draft}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|12=draft|12=draft}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=draft|12=draft}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|12=draft|12=draft}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft|12=draft}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|12=draft|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft|12=draft}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=draft|12=draft}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=draft|12=draft}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979th year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|12=draft|12=draft}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|12=draft|12=draft}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|12=draft|12=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=draft|12=draft}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft|12=draft}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|12=draft|12=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|12=draft|12=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|12=draft|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== References ==
<references />
== External Links == * [https://dailyportalz.jp/b/2007/05/09/c/ Myōkenjima on Daily Portal Z] * [https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/24985/ Urban Life Metro article on Myōkenjima]

At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|12=draft|12=draft}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|12=draft|12=draft}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|12=draft|12=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=draft|12=draft}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft|12=draft}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|12=draft|12=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|12=draft|12=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|12=draft|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

<references />

[[ja:妙見島]] [[sn:Myōken Island]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|12=draft|12=draft}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=draft|12=draft}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|12=draft|12=draft}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=draft|12=draft}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|12=draft|12=draft}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft|12=draft}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|12=draft|12=draft}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=draft|12=draft}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=draft|12=draft}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=draft|12=draft}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979th year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft|12=draft}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=draft|12=draft}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|12=draft|12=draft}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|12=draft|12=draft}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|12=draft|12=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=draft|12=draft}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft|12=draft}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|12=draft|12=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|12=draft|12=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft|12=draft}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|12=draft|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

revision.comment: 62 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:妙見島 up to rev 100161585
revision.text:
{{Infobox 島 |島名=妙見島 |画像=[[画像:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] |画像説明 = 上空から見た妙見島。[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）。{{smaller|{{国土航空写真}}}} |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 40|緯度秒 = 4 |経度度 = 139|経度分 = 53|経度秒 = 8 |面積= |周囲= |標高=3 |海域=[[旧江戸川]]（[[中州]]）{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}} |国= [[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[東葛西]]3丁目{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}} |地図2=Tokyo city |OSMズーム = 14 }} [[画像:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|[[歌川広重]] [[名所江戸百景]]「利根川ばらばらまつ」。「利根川」は現在の[[江戸川]]（[[旧江戸川]]）を指す。この絵は 『名所江戸百景』の中で唯一、場所が特定できないことで有名だが、妙見島付近から上流方面を望んだ構図というのが有力な説になっている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>。]] '''妙見島'''（みょうけんじま）は[[旧江戸川]]の島（[[中州]]）{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。南北の幅は約700[[メートル]]、東西の幅は約200メートル{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。[[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[東葛西]]3丁目の一部で、すぐ東側の旧江戸川の水面を[[県境|都県境]]が通る{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。
[[東京23区]]内に於いて唯一の自然島とされるが、異論もある<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） {{!}} アーバン ライフ メトロ - URBAN LIFE METRO - ULM |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 島の周囲は[[1979年]]（昭和54年）以降、すべて壁のように[[コンクリート]]護岸で囲まれて[[軍艦]]のような外観になっている{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />。護岸工事が行われるまでは「流れる島」として知られ、川の流れで北端が削られて南端に土砂が堆積して徐々に下流へと移動していた<ref name=":0" />。明治初期の地図上では、島は現在の位置よりも約80メートル上流にあった<ref name=":0" />。
現況は産業廃棄物業者の工場やマリーナなどが十数軒建ち並び、社員寮などで暮らす数十人が生活する<ref name=":0" />。北部に[[妙見神社 (東京都)|妙見神社]]<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>がある{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。[[浦安橋]]から島に降りることができる。<!--元は江戸川区側の岸と接していたが、島が徐々に離れていき、現在の位置まで移動した。現在もほんの少しずつ[[浦安市]]側に移動しているといわれる。-->
[[東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|葛西橋通り]]を往来する車両は、「ただの工場地帯」として通過するばかりで、島であることに気付く人は少ない{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。島民はいるが、人の気配はほとんどなく、[[貨物自動車|トラック]]と[[ネコ]]の多い島である{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。
== 歴史 == 最古の記録は[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[1362年]]（[[貞治]]元年）。島に妙見堂が建立されたという記録がある{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|新編武蔵國風土記稿 葛飾郡東一之江村 妙見堂}}。近辺の[[猫実 (浦安市)|猫実]]も[[1417年]]（[[応永]]24年）頃には陸地化していたとされる<ref>[[湯浅治久]]によると[[千葉県]][[市川市]]の『法宣院文書』に「猫眞講」の記述がある。</ref>。[[14世紀]]後半の[[海水準変動|パリア海退]]などにより、河口の陸地化が進んだものと思われる。
[[下総国]]の[[国府]]を流れる[[江戸川|太日川]]の河口であり、交通の要所だった。付近には[[長島高城]]があったという説がある<ref>『小田原衆所領役帳』の解釈。『葛飾記』の城館説。</ref>。長島には長島湊があり、国府の[[外港]]の1つとして栄えた。また長島には[[1372年]]（[[文中]]元年/[[応安]]5年）に、[[香取神宮]]の河関（灯油料所）があったことが知られている<ref>『旧大禰宜家文書』「藤原長者二条師良宜写」</ref>。
[[江戸時代]]は下総国[[欠真間|欠真間村]]の飛び地であった{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。島の北は[[行徳船]]の航路になっており、江戸の庶民にとってなじみ深い地域だった。[[曲亭馬琴]]『[[南総里見八犬伝]]』の舞台としても登場する。また、江戸時代の景観図では島内に松林が確認できる<ref name=":0" />。
[[1895年]]（[[明治]]28年）、千葉県から東京都に編入{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。[[明治]]時代後半より工場が建ち始め、[[第一次世界大戦]]後の[[昭和]]初期には[[工業地帯]]化した{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。昭和中期にかけては漁業も盛んな地域で、島内に海苔製造場や貝の加工工場も営業していたが、[[1960年代]]には水質汚染で漁ができなくなった<ref name=":0" />。[[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）には浦安橋が開通している{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。
[[2011年]]10月14日放送の[[テレビ朝日]][[オールニッポン・ニュースネットワーク|系列]]『[[タモリ倶楽部]]』にて妙見島が取り上げられ、島内にある業務用マーガリンを主に生産する「[[月島食品工業]]東京工場」、妙見島の名前の由来ともなったとも言われる「妙見神社」、都内でも最大の会員制マリンクラブである「ニューポート江戸川」などが紹介された。
== 島名の由来 == 妙見とは[[千葉氏]]の守護神である[[妙見菩薩]]のこと{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。妙見信仰は千葉氏が信仰していた[[日蓮宗]]とも密接に関わる。日蓮宗は中山[[法華経寺]]を中心に布教が行われていた。妙見堂の建立は千葉氏が[[葛西]]地区へ進出する足がかりだったという説もある<ref>湯浅治久の説</ref>。
なお初代の妙見堂は東一之江村（現・[[江戸川区]][[一之江]]）の[[妙覚寺 (江戸川区)|妙覚寺]]に移されているが現存せず、昭和期に再建されたものが残る（[[妙覚寺 (江戸川区)#妙見堂|妙覚寺#妙見堂]]）。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 参考文献 == * 江戸川区教育委員会編『江戸川区の民俗4 葛西地区の民俗』 * [[葛飾区郷土と天文の博物館]]『東京低地の中世を考える』（[[1995年]]（[[平成]]7年））{{ISBN2|978-4626015082}} ** [[湯浅治久]]「東京低地と江戸湾交通」 * {{cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第6巻 [[新編武蔵風土記稿|新編武蔵國風土記稿]]2|chapter=巻ノ28葛飾郡ノ9東一之江村|id={{NDLJP|1214842/58}}|publisher=雄山閣|editor=蘆田伊人編|date=1929-8|page=101|ref={{sfnref|新編武蔵國風土記稿 葛飾郡東一之江村 妙見堂}}}} * {{cite book|和書|author=秋山忠右・中原淳<!--1947年生-->|title=知られざる日本の不思議百景 「県境」の秘密|publisher=[[PHP研究所]]|date=2009-12-25|page=229|isbn=978-4-569-77356-8|ref={{sfnref|秋山・中原|2009}}}}
== 外部リンク == * [https://dailyportalz.jp/b/2007/05/09/c/ 江戸川に浮かぶ妙見島]（[[デイリーポータルZ]]） * {{Wayback|url=http://www015.upp.so-net.ne.jp/gofukakusa/yuasa-haruhisa-tokyoteichi-02.htm|date=20061006220757|title=湯浅治久 「東京低地と江戸湾交通」（後半）}} * [https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/24985/ 産廃・ラブホテル・マリンクラブの奇妙な共存 旧江戸川の中州「妙見島」を歩く] | アーバン ライフ メトロ - URBAN LIFE METRO - ULM * [http://www.newportmarine.co.jp/ ニューポート江戸川] * [http://www.tsukishima.co.jp/ 月島食品工業株式会社]

revision.text:
{{Infobox 島 |島名=妙見島 |画像=[[画像:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] |画像説明 = 上空から見た妙見島。[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）。{{smaller|{{国土航空写真}}}} |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 40|緯度秒 = 4 |経度度 = 139|経度分 = 53|経度秒 = 8 |面積= |周囲= |標高=3 |海域=[[旧江戸川]]（[[中州]]）{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}} |国= [[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[東葛西]]3丁目{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}} |地図2=Tokyo city |OSMズーム = 14 }} [[画像:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|[[歌川広重]] [[名所江戸百景]]「利根川ばらばらまつ」。「利根川」は現在の[[江戸川]]（[[旧江戸川]]）を指す。この絵は 『名所江戸百景』の中で唯一、場所が特定できないことで有名だが、妙見島付近から上流方面を望んだ構図というのが有力な説になっている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>。]] '''妙見島'''（みょうけんじま）は[[旧江戸川]]の島（[[中州]]）{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。南北の幅は約700[[メートル]]、東西の幅は約200メートル{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。[[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[東葛西]]3丁目の一部で、すぐ東側の旧江戸川の水面を[[県境|都県境]]が通る{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。
[[東京23区]]内に於いて唯一の自然島とされるが、異論もある<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） {{!}} アーバン ライフ メトロ - URBAN LIFE METRO - ULM |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 島の周囲は[[1979年]]（昭和54年）以降、すべて壁のように[[コンクリート]]護岸で囲まれて[[軍艦]]のような外観になっている{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />。護岸工事が行われるまでは「流れる島」として知られ、川の流れで北端が削られて南端に土砂が堆積して徐々に下流へと移動していた<ref name=":0" />。明治初期の地図上では、島は現在の位置よりも約80メートル上流にあった<ref name=":0" />。
現況は産業廃棄物業者の工場やマリーナなどが十数軒建ち並び、社員寮などで暮らす数十人が生活する<ref name=":0" />。北部に[[妙見神社 (東京都)|妙見神社]]<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>がある{{sfn|秋山・中原|2009|p=222}}。[[浦安橋]]から島に降りることができる。<!--元は江戸川区側の岸と接していたが、島が徐々に離れていき、現在の位置まで移動した。現在もほんの少しずつ[[浦安市]]側に移動しているといわれる。-->

[[下総国]]の[[国府]]を流れる[[江戸川|太日川]]の河口であり、交通の要所だった。付近には[[長島高城]]があったという説がある<ref>『小田原衆所領役帳』の解釈。『葛飾記』の城館説。</ref>。長島には長島湊があり、国府の[[外港]]の1つとして栄えた。また長島には[[1372年]]（[[文中]]元年/[[応安]]5年）に、[[香取神宮]]の河関（灯油料所）があったことが知られている<ref>『旧大禰宜家文書』「藤原長者二条師良宜写」</ref>。

{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979th year}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.

At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=draft}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=draft}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=draft}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|12=draft}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|12=draft}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|12=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=draft}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|12=draft}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=draft}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|12=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|12=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|12=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

[[sn:Myōken Island]] [[ja:妙見島]]

{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|qq=draft}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都|qq=draft}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|qq=draft}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|qq=draft}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979th year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|qq=draft}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|qq=draft}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|qq=draft}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|qq=draft}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|qq=draft}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|qq=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|qq=draft}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|qq=draft}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|qq=draft}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|qq=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|qq=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|qq=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|qq=draft}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|qq=draft}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|qq=draft}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|qq=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|qq=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

[[sn:Myōken Island]] [[ja:妙見島]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:translated pages without meta categories]]\n" to "[[Category:translated pages without meta categories]] "
revision.text:
{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|qq=draft}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都|qq=draft}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|qq=draft}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|qq=draft}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979th year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|qq=draft}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|qq=draft}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|qq=draft}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|qq=draft}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|qq=draft}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|qq=draft}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|qq=draft}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|qq=draft}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|qq=draft}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|qq=draft}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|qq=draft}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== References ==
<references />
== External Links == * [https://dailyportalz.jp/b/2007/05/09/c/ Myōkenjima on Daily Portal Z] * [https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/24985/ Urban Life Metro article on Myōkenjima]

At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|qq=}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|qq=}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trucks|qq=}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cats|qq=}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1362|qq=}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōji|qq=}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|qq=}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1417|qq=}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa, Chiba|qq=}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|qq=}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimōsa Province|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|qq=}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokutei Bakin|qq=}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|qq=}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1895|qq=}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bodhisattva|qq=}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba clan|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

[[sn:Myōken Island]] [[ja:妙見島]]

{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|qq=}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|qq=}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandbank|qq=}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolis|qq=}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edogawa, Tokyo|qq=}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|qq=}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utagawa Hiroshige|qq=}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qq=}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo River|qq=}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|qq=}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandbank|qq=}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|qq=}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|qq=}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edogawa, Tokyo|qq=}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|qq=}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1979|qq=}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|qq=}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|qq=}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trucks|qq=}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cats|qq=}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1362|qq=}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōji|qq=}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|qq=}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1417|qq=}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa, Chiba|qq=}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|qq=}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimōsa Province|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|qq=}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokutei Bakin|qq=}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|qq=}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1895|qq=}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bodhisattva|qq=}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba clan|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1895|qq=}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bodhisattva|qq=}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba clan|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

[[sn:Myōken Island]] [[ja:妙見島]]

{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|qq=}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|qq=}} ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandbank|qq=}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo Metropolis|ja|東京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolis|qq=}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edogawa, Tokyo|qq=}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|qq=}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utagawa Hiroshige|qq=}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qq=}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|ja|江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo River|qq=}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|qq=}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandbank|qq=}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|qq=}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|qq=}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edogawa, Tokyo|qq=}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|qq=}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1979|qq=}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|qq=}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|qq=}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trucks|qq=}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cats|qq=}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1362|qq=}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōji|qq=}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|qq=}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1417|qq=}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa, Chiba|qq=}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|qq=}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimōsa Province|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|qq=}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokutei Bakin|qq=}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|qq=}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1895|qq=}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bodhisattva|qq=}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba clan|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|Sandbank|ja|中州|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandbank|qq=}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|qq=}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|qq=}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|ja|江戸川区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edogawa, Tokyo|qq=}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|qq=}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|ja|1979年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1979|qq=}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|qq=}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|qq=}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Trucks|ja|貨物自動車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trucks|qq=}} and stray {{ill|Cats|ja|ネコ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cats|qq=}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|ja|1362年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1362|qq=}} ({{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōji|qq=}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|qq=}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|ja|1417年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1417|qq=}} ({{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei|qq=}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|ja|市川市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikawa, Chiba|qq=}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|qq=}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimōsa Province|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|qq=}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Kyokutei Bakin|ja|曲亭馬琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokutei Bakin|qq=}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|ja|南総里見八犬伝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|qq=}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|ja|1895年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1895|qq=}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken Bodhisattva|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bodhisattva|qq=}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba clan|qq=}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|qq=}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.

Since [[:en:1979|1979]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|qq=}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more [[:en:Truck|Trucks]] and stray [[:en:Cat|Cats]] than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to [[:en:1362|1362]] ([[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|qq=}} in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In [[:en:1895|1895]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bodhisattva]], the guardian deity of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
