Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more [[:en:Truck|Trucks]] and stray [[:en:Cat|Cats]] than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to [[:en:1362|1362]] ([[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Nekozane, Urayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around [[:en:1417|1417]] ([[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from [[:en:Ichikawa, Chiba|Ichikawa, Chiba]], Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in [[:en:Takizawa Bakin|Kyokutei Bakin]]'s *[[:en:Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden]]*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In [[:en:1895|1895]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bodhisattva]], the guardian deity of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] [[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]] "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day [[:en:Edo River|Edo River]] ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ([[:en:River island|Sandbank]]) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome, [[:en:Edogawa, Tokyo|Edogawa, Tokyo]], Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=ward area of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since [[:en:1979|1979]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more [[:en:Truck|Trucks]] and stray [[:en:Cat|Cats]] than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to [[:en:1362|1362]] ([[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Nekozane, Urayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around [[:en:1417|1417]] ([[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from [[:en:Ichikawa, Chiba|Ichikawa, Chiba]], Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in [[:en:Takizawa Bakin|Kyokutei Bakin]]'s *[[:en:Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden]]*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In [[:en:1895|1895]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bodhisattva]], the guardian deity of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[sn:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11447030]] Wikidata name: Myōken Island [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei]]) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}} ([[:en:River island|Sandbank]]){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo Metropolis]] [[:en:Edogawa, Tokyo|Edogawa, Tokyo]] {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] [[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]] "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day [[:en:Edo River|Edo River]] ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ([[:en:River island|Sandbank]]) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome, [[:en:Edogawa, Tokyo|Edogawa, Tokyo]], Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=ward area of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since [[:en:1979|1979]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more [[:en:Truck|Trucks]] and stray [[:en:Cat|Cats]] than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to [[:en:1362|1362]] ([[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Nekozane, Urayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around [[:en:1417|1417]] ([[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from [[:en:Ichikawa, Chiba|Ichikawa, Chiba]], Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in [[:en:Takizawa Bakin|Kyokutei Bakin]]'s *[[:en:Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden]]*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In [[:en:1895|1895]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bodhisattva]], the guardian deity of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]

== Description ==
Since [[:en:1979|1979]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more [[:en:Truck|Trucks]] and stray [[:en:Cat|Cats]] than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to [[:en:1362|1362]] ([[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Nekozane, Urayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around [[:en:1417|1417]] ([[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from [[:en:Ichikawa, Chiba|Ichikawa, Chiba]], Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in [[:en:Takizawa Bakin|Kyokutei Bakin]]'s *[[:en:Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden]]*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In [[:en:1895|1895]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bodhisattva]], the guardian deity of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]]{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] [[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]] "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day [[:en:Edo River|Edo River]] ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ([[:en:River island|Sandbank]]) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome, [[:en:Edogawa, Tokyo|Edogawa, Tokyo]], Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=ward area of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.

Since [[:en:1979|1979]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.

Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more [[:en:Truck|Trucks]] and stray [[:en:Cat|Cats]] than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[co:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[co:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[sn:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11447030]] Wikidata name: Myōken Island [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}} ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo Metropolis}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|WD=Q200798|lt=Utagawa Hiroshige}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|WD=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|en|Edo River|WD=Q1194505|lt=Edo River}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=ward area of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|en|1979|WD=Q2484|lt=1979}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Truck|en|Truck|WD=Q43193|lt=Trucks}} and stray {{ill|Cat|en|Cat|WD=Q146|lt=Cats}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|en|1362|WD=Q6166|lt=1362}} ({{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Nekozane, Urayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|en|1417|WD=Q6193|lt=1417}} ({{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, Chiba|WD=Q209785|lt=Ichikawa, Chiba}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|WD=Q463142|lt=Kyokutei Bakin}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|en|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|WD=Q557852|lt=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== References ==
<references />
== External Links == * [https://dailyportalz.jp/b/2007/05/09/c/ Myōkenjima on Daily Portal Z] * [https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/24985/ Urban Life Metro article on Myōkenjima] {{translated page|ja|妙見島|version=100161585|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ceb:My%C5%8Dken Jima]]

This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=ward area of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|en|1979|WD=Q2484|lt=1979}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Truck|en|Truck|WD=Q43193|lt=Trucks}} and stray {{ill|Cat|en|Cat|WD=Q146|lt=Cats}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|en|1362|WD=Q6166|lt=1362}} ({{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Nekozane, Urayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|en|1417|WD=Q6193|lt=1417}} ({{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, Chiba|WD=Q209785|lt=Ichikawa, Chiba}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|WD=Q463142|lt=Kyokutei Bakin}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|en|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|WD=Q557852|lt=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

On early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Truck|en|Truck|WD=Q43193|lt=Trucks}} and stray {{ill|Cat|en|Cat|WD=Q146|lt=Cats}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|en|1362|WD=Q6166|lt=1362}} ({{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Nekozane, Urayasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|en|1417|WD=Q6193|lt=1417}} ({{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, Chiba|WD=Q209785|lt=Ichikawa, Chiba}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|WD=Q463142|lt=Kyokutei Bakin}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|en|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|WD=Q557852|lt=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[co:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[co:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[sn:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]]
{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397|1=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}} ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank|1=River island}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo Metropolis|1=Tokyo}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|WD=Q200798|lt=Utagawa Hiroshige|1=Hiroshige}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|WD=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|en|Edo River|WD=Q1194505|lt=Edo River|1=Edo River}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank|1=River island}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=Special wards of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|en|1979|WD=Q2484|lt=1979|1=1979}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Truck|en|Truck|WD=Q43193|lt=Trucks|1=Truck}} and stray {{ill|Cat|en|Cat|WD=Q146|lt=Cats|1=Cat}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|en|1362|WD=Q6166|lt=1362|1=1362}} ({{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji|1=Jōji}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Q11572007|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|en|1417|WD=Q6193|lt=1417|1=1417}} ({{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei|1=Ōei}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, Chiba|WD=Q209785|lt=Ichikawa, Chiba|1=Ichikawa, Chiba}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|WD=Q463142|lt=Kyokutei Bakin|1=Takizawa Bakin}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|en|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|WD=Q557852|lt=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|1=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva|1=Myōken}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan|1=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Q11572007|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|en|1417|WD=Q6193|lt=1417|1=1417}} ({{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei|1=Ōei}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, Chiba|WD=Q209785|lt=Ichikawa, Chiba|1=Ichikawa, Chiba}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|WD=Q463142|lt=Kyokutei Bakin|1=Takizawa Bakin}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|en|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|WD=Q557852|lt=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|1=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva|1=Myōken}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan|1=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

<references />

[[ceb:My%C5%8Dken Jima]]
[[ja:妙見島]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ceb:Myōken Jima]] {{Commons category|Myōken-jima, Edogawa}} {{wikidata link|Q11447030}} [[Category:Chiba Clan]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Geography of Edogawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Islands of Tokyo]] [[Category:Kasai, Edogawa]] [[Category:River islands]] [[Category:Tone River system]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[co:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[co:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]]
{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397|1=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}} ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank|1=River island}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo Metropolis|1=Tokyo}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|WD=Q200798|lt=Utagawa Hiroshige|1=Hiroshige}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|WD=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|en|Edo River|WD=Q1194505|lt=Edo River|1=Edo River}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank|1=River island}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=Special wards of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|en|1979|WD=Q2484|lt=1979|1=1979}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Truck|en|Truck|WD=Q43193|lt=Trucks|1=Truck}} and stray {{ill|Cat|en|Cat|WD=Q146|lt=Cats|1=Cat}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|en|1362|WD=Q6166|lt=1362|1=1362}} ({{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji|1=Jōji}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Q11572007|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|en|1417|WD=Q6193|lt=1417|1=1417}} ({{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei|1=Ōei}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, Chiba|WD=Q209785|lt=Ichikawa, Chiba|1=Ichikawa, Chiba}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|WD=Q463142|lt=Kyokutei Bakin|1=Takizawa Bakin}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|en|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|WD=Q557852|lt=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|1=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva|1=Myōken}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan|1=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

<references />

The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|en|Edo River|WD=Q1194505|lt=Edo River|1=Edo River}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}). In {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva|1=Myōken}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan|1=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

[[ja:妙見島]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ceb:Myōken Jima]] {{Commons category|Myōken-jima, Edogawa}} {{wikidata link|Q11447030}} [[Category:chiba clan]] [[Category:Geography of Edogawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Islands of Tokyo]] [[Category:Kasai, Edogawa]] [[Category:River islands]] [[Category:Tone River system]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[co:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[co:commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Myōken Jima]][[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Myōken-jima, Edogawa]][[ja:妙見島]]
{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397|1=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}} ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank|1=River island}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo Metropolis|1=Tokyo}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|WD=Q200798|lt=Utagawa Hiroshige|1=Hiroshige}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|WD=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|en|Edo River|WD=Q1194505|lt=Edo River|1=Edo River}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|River island|en|River island|WD=Q162602|lt=Sandbank|1=River island}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528297}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=Special wards of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|en|1979|WD=Q2484|lt=1979|1=1979}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Truck|en|Truck|WD=Q43193|lt=Trucks|1=Truck}} and stray {{ill|Cat|en|Cat|WD=Q146|lt=Cats|1=Cat}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|en|1362|WD=Q6166|lt=1362|1=1362}} ({{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji|1=Jōji}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Q11572007|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|en|1417|WD=Q6193|lt=1417|1=1417}} ({{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei|1=Ōei}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, Chiba|WD=Q209785|lt=Ichikawa, Chiba|1=Ichikawa, Chiba}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|WD=Q463142|lt=Kyokutei Bakin|1=Takizawa Bakin}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|en|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|WD=Q557852|lt=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|1=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva|1=Myōken}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan|1=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.

The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|en|Edo River|qid=Q1194505|lt=Edo River|1=Edo River}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]

{{Infobox island | name = Myōkenjima (妙見島) | image = [[File:Myoukenjima_Edogawa.jpg|200px]] | caption = Aerial view of Myōkenjima, taken in [[1989]] ({{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|qid=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}) 1. {{smaller|{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135491397|1=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}}} | latitude_deg = 35 | latitude_min = 40 | latitude_sec = 4 | longitude_deg = 139 | longitude_min = 53 | longitude_sec = 8 | area = | perimeter = | elevation = 3 | body_of_water = {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11511084}} ({{ill|River island|en|River island|qid=Q162602|lt=Sandbank|1=River island}}){{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | country = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo Metropolis|1=Tokyo}} {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|qid=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}} {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11528297}} 3-chome{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}} | map2 = Tokyo city | osm_zoom = 14 }}
[[File:100 views edo 061.jpg|thumb|120px|{{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|qid=Q200798|lt=Utagawa Hiroshige|1=Hiroshige}} {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}} "Tonegawa Barabara Matsu". The "Tonegawa" here refers to the present-day {{ill|Edo River|en|Edo River|qid=Q1194505|lt=Edo River|1=Edo River}} ({{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11511084}}). This image is famous for being the only one in *One Hundred Famous Views of Edo* where the exact location remains unidentified, but a leading theory suggests it depicts an upstream view from around Myōkenjima.<ref>{{Cite web|title=『利根川ばらばらまつ』：浮世写真家 喜千也の「名所江戸百景」第70回 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/guide-to-japan/gu004070/ |website=nippon.com |date=2020-08-09 |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.]]
== Overview ==
Myōkenjima (妙見島) is an island ({{ill|River island|en|River island|qid=Q162602|lt=Sandbank|1=River island}}) in the {{ill|Old Edo River|ja|旧江戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Edo River|1=Kyū-Edo River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11511084}}. It measures approximately 700 meters north to south and 200 meters east to west{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. It is part of {{ill|Higashikasai|ja|東葛西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashikasai|1=Higashi-Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11528297}} 3-chome, {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|qid=Q214056|lt=Edogawa, Tokyo|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}}, Tokyo, with the metropolitan border running through the water of the Old Edo River just east of the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
It is often claimed to be the only *natural island* within {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|ja|東京23区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special wards of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都区部|1=Special wards of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q308891}}, but there are some disputes regarding this claim<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=＜望 ～都の空から＞妙見島 姿を変えた「流れる島」：東京新聞 TOKYO Web |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/134541 |website=東京新聞 TOKYO Web |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=江戸川区「妙見島」は本当に「23区唯一の自然島」なのか？ 流布された通説を検証する【連載】東京うしろ髪ひかれ地帯（14） |url=https://urbanlife.tokyo/post/65350/ |website=アーバン ライフ メトロ |accessdate=2022-04-09 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== Description ==
Since {{ill|1979|en|1979|qid=Q2484|lt=1979|1=1979}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54), the entire perimeter of the island has been surrounded by concrete embankments, giving it an appearance reminiscent of a battleship{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}<ref name=":0" />. Before these embankments were built, the island was known as a "drifting island" because the river's flow eroded its northern edge while depositing sediment on the southern end, gradually shifting the island downstream<ref name=":0" />. On early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} maps, the island appears about 80 meters upstream from its present location<ref name=":0" />.
Currently, the island is occupied by several industrial waste disposal plants, marinas, and employee dormitories, with a few dozen residents<ref name=":0" />. At the northern part of the island stands {{ill|Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|妙見神社 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>[http://shrine.iki-kiru.com/myoken/ 妙見神社（妙見島） - 神社散歩]</ref>{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Access to the island is available via {{ill|Urayasu Bridge|ja|浦安橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu Bridge|1=Urayasu Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11558213}}.
Vehicles passing along {{ill|Tokyo Route 10|ja|東京都道・千葉県道10号東京浦安線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Route 10|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road and Chiba Prefectural Road Route 10|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11525843}} (Kasaibashi-dōri) tend to overlook the island, assuming it is merely an industrial area{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. Although there are inhabitants, the island has little human presence, with more {{ill|Truck|en|Truck|qid=Q43193|lt=Trucks|1=Truck}} and stray {{ill|Cat|en|Cat|qid=Q146|lt=Cats|1=Cat}} than people{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== History ==
The earliest recorded mention of Myōkenjima dates back to {{ill|1362|en|1362|qid=Q6166|lt=1362|1=1362}} ({{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|qid=Q1146153|lt=Jōji|1=Jōji}} 1), when a Myōken Hall (妙見堂) was built on the island{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}{{sfn|New-ly Edited Musashi Province Topography|ja|新編武蔵國風土記稿}}. The nearby {{ill|Nekozane, Urayasu|ja|猫実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nekozane, Urayasu|1=Q11572007|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11572007}} was also landfilled around {{ill|1417|en|1417|qid=Q6193|lt=1417|1=1417}} ({{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|qid=Q1193078|lt=Ōei|1=Ōei}} 24)<ref>[[Yuasaharuhisa]] notes that a reference to "Neko Shrine Society" (猫眞講) appears in the *Hōsen-in Archives* (法宣院文書) from {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, Chiba|qid=Q209785|lt=Ichikawa, Chiba|1=Ichikawa, Chiba}}, Chiba Prefecture.</ref>. This land development is believed to have been influenced by 14th-century sea level changes.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Myōkenjima was a detached landholding of {{ill|Ketsuma|ja|欠真間村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ketsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|qid=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}. The northern part of the island was a key route for {{ill|Gyotoku Ships|ja|行徳船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyotoku Ships|1=Gyotoku Ships|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11626069}}, making it well-known to Edo residents. It is also mentioned as a setting in {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|qid=Q463142|lt=Kyokutei Bakin|1=Takizawa Bakin}}'s *{{ill|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|en|Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|qid=Q557852|lt=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden|1=Nansō Satomi Hakkenden}}*. Additionally, Edo-period illustrations show pine forests on the island<ref name=":0" />.
In {{ill|1895|en|1895|qid=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 28), the island was incorporated into Tokyo from Chiba Prefecture{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== Origin of the Name ==
The name "Myōken" originates from {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bodhisattva|1=Myōken}}, the guardian deity of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|qid=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan|1=Chiba clan}}{{sfn|Akiyama & Nakahara|2009|p=222}}.
== References ==
<references />

[[ceb:My%C5%8Dken Jima]]
